Title: Touching Virtual Humans: Haptic Responses Reveal the Emotional Impact of Affective Agents
Author: Ahmed, Imtiaj and Harjunen, Ville and Jacucci, Giulio and Ravaja, Niklas and Ruotsalo, Tuukka and Spapé, Michiel
C2.1_Haptic_device_(F): An input device similar to a blood pressure measure.
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: We studied whether the emotional expression of a virtual agent would affect the way humans touch the agent.
C3.2.1_Levels:
- Agents (4, 2 males/2 females)
- Emotion expressed by agent (7)
- Variation in emotion expression (3)
-> 84 combinations repeated twice
C3.4_Hypotheses_description:
H1. The agents' emotional expressions affect users' affective states.
H2. The agents' emotional expressions affect users' haptic responses.
H3. The agents' emotional cues have consistent haptic responses from users.
Concluding statement: "Our study shows that the way we touch a virtual agent’s arm is affected by how we feel, and how we perceive the agent to feel."
CS code: Social touch, Virtual agent, Affect, Social perception
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: "Even a basic, affordable haptic interface is thus shown to provide remarkably rich information on a user’s ongoing emotional state."
Benefit_code: Haptic can convey emotional information during interaction
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: Our haptic input device was designed using low cost components obtained from a commercial blood pressure measurement device with limited capability to detect haptic features.
C4.4_Future work: Similar pressure sensors could be used to infer a person’s emotional reactions and stress level in various demanding tasks.
Future_work_code: Need to test on more complex task

Title: Object manipulation in visuo-haptic augmented reality with physics-based animation
Author: Aleotti, Jacopo and Denaro, Francesco and Caselli, Stefano
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: The paper proposes an approach for physics-based AR with haptic interaction to improve the efficiency in the virtual environment.
C3.4_Hypotheses_description: Force feedback should help the user and allow faster and more effective performance even in AR tasks
Concluding statement: Experiments have confirmed that force feedback reduces task completion time.
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: Force feedback complements visual feedback and facilitates interaction when the virtual proxy is visually occluded, providing additional information when visual is unavailable.
Benefit_code: Haptic improves interaction in VE.
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: N/A
C4.4_Future work: N/A

Title: Effects of network delay on a collaborative motor task with telehaptic and televisual feedback
Author: Allison, Robert S. and Zacher, James E. and Wang, David and Shu, Joseph
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: In this study we consider the effects of varying amounts of simulated constant delay on the performance of a simple collaborative haptic task.
Concluding statement: "In this study we demonstrated that delays, typical of those encountered in long distance telecollaboration, inhibit the ability of users to collaborate using haptic or visual communication at a distance."
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: "We have shown that a cooperative task can be performed based solely on haptic communication between the users even in the presence of simulated network delay."
Benefit_code: Haptic information is sufficient for cooperative task
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: "In virtual environments and tele-operators, latency is universally detrimental resulting in mismatch between motor action and simulated sensory feedback."
C4.4_Future work: "In general, it may also be necessary to consider the effects of the signal processing involved in predictive filtering." -> Delay

Title: Intermodal audio–haptic intermodal display: improvement of communication and interpersonal awareness for collaborative search tasks
Author: Ammi, Mehdi and Katz, Brian
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: The paper studies a new sensorial communication strategy based on the development of a spatial binaural audio-ìhaptic intermodal display which aims at improving communication and working efficiency during synchronous collaborative tasks.
C3.3.1_DVs: Code Performance etc:
Performance: Time, Score
Behavior: Social interaction
C3.4_Hypotheses_description:
H1. The intermodal display improves overall performance.
H2. The intermodal display reduces the need for verbal.
H3. The use of the intermodal display is easy to understand communication between partners.
C3.4.1_Hypotheses_code: Multimodal is better than haptic alone.
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: Intermodal (haptic and audio) display play the role of a communication tool reduce the need of verbal communication. Improve target searching (time and accuracy).
Benefit_code: Haptic and audio can reduce the need of verbal.
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: the approach introduces some communication conflicts during steps presenting high coupling between partners what reduces the efficiency of the group.
C4.4_Future work: addition of low-amplitude harmonic components which can be varied in order to provide both audio and haptic variations while maintaining the same fundamental frequencies.

Title: I’m in control! Transferring object ownership between remote users with haptic props in virtual reality
Author: Auda, Jonas and Busse, Leon and Pfeuffer, Ken and Gruenefeld, Uwe and Rivu, Radiah and Alt, Florian and Schneegass, Stefan
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: We explored how taking over ownership of virtual objects can influence collaboration.
Concluding statement: "We explored different ways to use haptic props in VR for remote collaboration."
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: "We explored how collaborators can use haptic props to share knowledge if they cannot take over ownership of virtual objects."
Benefit_code: Proposed interaction technique improves communication with haptic props
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: "We investigate haptic props with a particular form factor and made of a particular material. [...] [Collaboration] may be perceived differently when the size of haptic props and virtual objects differ."
Limitation_code: Single haptic prop lacks of diversity
C4.4_Future work: "Future investigations could focus on enabling more generic forms [for haptic props] that make use of dynamically fabricated or even shape-changing objects."

Title: Virtual interpersonal touch: Haptic interaction and copresence in collaborative virtual environments
Author: Bailenson, Jeremy and Yee, Nick
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: The current study had two goals. The first was to examine how people virtually touch representations of other people inside of CVEs. The second goal was to attempt to use haptic devices as a benchmark for copresence.
Concluding statement: People interact haptically with virtual people in a measurably different manner from other nonhuman objects.
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: Haptic feedback can be a behavioral metric for co-presence
Benefit_code: Haptic as a metric
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: The task is not a real social touch
C4.4_Future work: Explore different social touch scenarios.

Title: An experimental study on the role of touch in shared virtual environments
Author: Basdogan, Cagatay and Ho, Chih-Hao and Srinivasan, Mandayam A. and Slater, Mel
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: The goals of the study are (1) to assess the impact of force feedback on task performance, (2) to better understand the role of haptic communication in human-human interactions, (3) to study the impact of touch on the subjective sense of collaborating [...], and (4) to investigate if gender, personality, or emotional experiences of users can affect haptic communication in SVEs.
C3.3.2_DVs: Code self-reports: Social presence, Performance, Affect, Collaboration, Personality
Concluding statement: "This paper reports the results of an experimental study conducted to examine the extent to which haptic communication, in addition to the usual visual feedback, influences the task performance and sense of togetherness between remote participants, subject’s perception of the remote partner’s gender, and expert behavior (which includes social anxiety and self-confidence, dexterity, and patience) in an SVE."
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: "The results obtained suggest that haptic feedback affects significantly the task performance and the sense of togetherness."
Benefit_code: Haptic influences collaborative performance and experience
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: "The haptic interaction model used in this study [...] was simple"
C4.4_Future work: "A more sophisticated interaction paradigm can be developed to understand the underlying motor control, cognitive, and perceptual processes that occur during these interactions."

Title: Supporting parent-child collaborative learning through haptic feedback displays
Author: Beheshti, Elham and Borgos-Rodriguez, Katya and Piper, Anne Marie
C2.1_Haptic_device_(F): Tanvas Tablet, a variable friction haptic display.
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: The paper investigates how children perceive haptic feedback and how these experiences should be designed for collaborative learning.
Concluding statement: Our analysis shows that dyads in the haptic condition performed slightly better when predicting their answers to learning tasks.
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: Enhance collaborative experiences.
Benefit_code: Haptic enhances collaborative experiences
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: However, we found that haptic feedback introduced new complexities for how dyads perceived and discussed the exhibit content.
Limitation_code: Participants need to learn the haptic communication.
C4.4_Future work: How haptics affect collaborative learning.

Title: Impact of Multimodal Instructions for Tool Manipulation Skills on Performance and User Experience in an Immersive Environment
Author: C. Simon and M. Boukli-Hacene and F. Lebrun and S. Otmane and A. Chellali
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: How a teacher can effictively assist learners in achieving the correct amplitude of tool movements using multimodal instructions? Which combination of modalities is best suited for [instruction delivery] considering performance and user experiences?
C3.4_Hypotheses_description:
H1. Visual haptic: H+V and H+V+A would have best performance
H2. Mental workload decreases with V+H+A instead of bimodal conditions
H3. Audio: A+H and A+V have better UX w/ self-reports
C3.4.1_Hypotheses_code: More modalities increase the performance and experience of haptic collaboration
Concluding statement: #NAME?
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in:
V+H was best for performance, 
V+H+A was best preferred
Benefit_code: Multimodal is better than single sensory input.
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: Learning was not considered in the study
Limitation_code: Participants need to learn the haptic communication.
C4.4_Future work: How multimodal influences the instructor's experience.

Title: Incomplete information about the partner affects the development of collaborative strategies in joint action
Author: Chackochan, Vinil and Sanguineti, Vittorio
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: How would the collaboration be affected if information about their partner were unreliable or incomplete?
C3.4_Hypotheses_description:
H1. Performance improves with time
H2. Performance is affected by Feedback about partner
Concluding statement: "We found that incomplete information about the partner affects not only the speed at which collaboration is achieved (less information, slower learning), but also the actual collaboration strategy."
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: N/A
Benefit_code: N/A
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: Insufficient information to figure out the partner's goals
Limitation_code: Require more information to infer user's intention
C4.4_Future work: "Future experiments, possibly involving generalization to other tasks or interacting with a virtual partner will be necessary to clarify this important point."

Title: Designing haptic icons to support collaborative turn-taking
Author: Chan, Andrew and MacLean, Karon and McGrenere, Joanna
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: This paper describes research exploring the use of haptics to support users collaborating remotely in a single-user shared application. [...] We therefore designed a custom turn-taking protocol that allows users to express different levels of urgency in their request for control from a collaborator; state of control and requests are communicated by touch, with the intent of offloading visual attention.
C3.3.1_DVs: Code Performance etc:
Performance: Score, Efficiency
Behavior: Social interaction
C3.4_Hypotheses_description: Haptic feedback could play a role in conveying urgency of a user’s desire for control.
Concluding statement: "In summary, we have observed concrete support for our theory that haptic mediation can enhance usability both perceptually and socially, because its use of a different sensory modality than the primary task makes it less disruptive."
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: "haptics could help to support non-verbal communication among distributed collaborators."
Benefit_code: Haptic feedback improves collaborative performance and experiences
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: "Socially, we have identified issues of perceived intimacy and etiquette which need to be kept in mind when designing interactions in which unassertive users can be more comfortable in requesting control, and domineering users more aware of others’ needs."
C4.4_Future work: "The potential utility of lightweight haptic signals and our urgency-based protocol extends to other categories of distributed collaboration and shared control over resources or a group’s attention, including co-located settings where it can be awkward to get the attention of a dominating participant."
Future_work_code: Tge proposde technique can be used for notification.

Title: Influences of haptic communication on a shared manual task
Author: Chellali, Amine and Dumas, Cédric and Milleville-Pennel, Isabelle
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: Our objective is to show that supporting functional haptic interactions in CVE can improve the users' collaborative performance in such tasks.
C3.4_Hypotheses_description: We hypothesize that a CVE that supports different interactions channels helps the partners to exchange more accurate information about their common manual tasks and enhances the collaborative performance.
Concluding statement: "The results show that the haptic communication training improves the users’ accuracy when they perform in dyads the shared manual task."
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: "the haptic communication training enhanced the users feeling of copresence with the partner in the virtual environment and increased the users’ perception of the system quality."
Benefit_code: Haptic feedback improves collaborative performance and experiences
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback:
"Underexplored" (?)
N/A
C4.4_Future work: "In future, a deeper verbalization analysis is needed to explore more accurately the development of common ground between partners."
Future_work_code: Explore how collaborators form their common ground.

Title: Performance related energy exchange in haptic human-human interaction in a shared virtual object manipulation task
Author: D. Feth and R. Groten and A. Peer and S. Hirche and M. Buss
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: In this paper the effect of haptic interaction in single and dyadic conditions is investigated.
C3.3.1_DVs: Code Performance etc:
Performance: Score, Errors
Behavior: Force applied
C3.4_Hypotheses_description:
We expect tracking performance to be better in partner
condition than in single condition.
Concluding statement: "we can confirm our hypothesis that performance is increased in the [grouped] condition."
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: N/A
Benefit_code: N/A
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: N/A
C4.4_Future work: "Because interaction in the [grouped] condition was even better than in the [alone/half effort] condition, it is concluded that the improved task performance in dyadic trials is not only a result of force reduction for the individual but different explanations have to be considered."
Future_work_code: Explore the group dynamic during collaboration.

Title: On Multi-Agent Cognitive Cooperation: Can virtual agents behave like humans?
Author: D’Avella, Salvatore and Camacho-Gonzalez, Gerardo and Tripicchio, Paolo
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: The paper studies the interaction among two players in a virtual environment that, by means of haptic devices and cognitive collaboration processes, have to escape a maze with complementary information at their disposal to achieve the task.
Concluding statement: The experiments demonstrate that a multimodal channel consisting of vision and haptics allows establishing a common frame of reference (CRF) between the two participants for learning how to collaborate to accomplish a shared task.
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: Haptic feedback supports participants to build a common frame of reference, but the study does not an IV so no comparaison without.
Benefit_code: Haptic feedback allows for building a common ground
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: Initial difficulty to understand the role of haptic feedback and how to use the device
C4.4_Future work: N/A

Title: Assessment of an articulatory interface for tactile intercommunication in immersive virtual environments
Author: de Jesus Oliveira, Victor Adriel and Nedel, Luciana and Maciel, Anderson
C2.1_Haptic_device_(F): Custom vibrotactile interface (mounted on a HMD)
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: The paper designs and assesses a touch user interface that is built on a Head-Mounted Display (HMD) to allow peers to exchange tactile signals in collaborative VR applications.
C3.2.1_Levels: Direction, positive, negative, Near R/L, Far R/L
Concluding statement: Subjects presented high accuracy for interpreting tactile signals and directly applied the knowledge about the vibrotactile vocabulary into its articulation. Our contribution is the design and assessment of an interface made for tactile intercommunication in virtual applications, for close or remote tasks, with or without the assistance of other sensory modalities.
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: straightforward mapping between action and sensation (to touch and to be touched) provided an easy way for interlocutors to learn and to apply the tactile communication in the immersive setup.
Benefit_code: Haptic feedback allows for building a common ground
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: ok for simple task but could be insufficient in complex task/ latency/ gesture unprecision can change tactile signal/ could not work in noisy or stressfull environment
C4.4_Future work: study impact of fatigue/ pressure sensor to enrich tactile communication/ specific audience (visually impaired ...)

Title: Proactive haptic articulation for intercommunication in collaborative virtual environments
Author: de Jesus Oliveira, Victor and Nedel, Luciana and Maciel, Anderson
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: This paper studies how haptic feedback can establish a new communication chanel to in a collaborative puzzle task.
C3.4_Hypotheses_description: Haptic feedback can be a medium for communication as verbal.
Concluding statement: Participants adapted the vocabulary of haptic feedback in their communication during the collaborative task.
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: Haptic is an other way of communication
Benefit_code: Haptic feedback can be an alternative for communication
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: N/A
C4.4_Future work: The paper wants to verify whether the changes in the vocabulary can converge towards a new final dialect during long conversations using tactile feedback.
Future_work_code: Explore how collaborators form their common ground.

Title: Usage of tactile feedback to assist cooperative object manipulations in virtual environments
Author: de Oliveira, Thomas and Pinho, Márcio
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: The paper evaluates the usage of tactile feedback to aid cooperative object manipulation using the SkeweR technique.
Concluding statement:
the use of visual or However, once the Apart event happens, the tactile
feedback showed itself as the most effective way to keep the
distance small, and to reduce the time of this event.
tactile feedback makes no significant difference on task
completion time.
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: Tactile feedback more efficient than visual feedback to reduce the distance between crushing point and virtual representation
Benefit_code: Efficiency
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: Physic discomfort of wearing vibration device/ hard to interpret the vibration intensity/
C4.4_Future work:
add a penalty for the users
when they collide the shared object against an obstacle or the
wire.
Future_work_code: Improve the expressiveness of haptic feedback

Title: Mentor-Guided Learning in Immersive Virtual Environments: The Impact of Visual and Haptic Feedback on Skill Acquisition
Author: F. Lebrun and C. Simon and A. Boukezzi and S. Otmane and A. Chellali
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: The main contribution of this work is to investigate the effectiveness of augmented feedback in an immersive CVE for transferring technical skills from an expert to a learner.
C3.4_Hypotheses_description:
H1. The augmented feedback (either uni or multimodal) received during the training improves the participants’ learning curve in terms of performance measures compared to the control group.
H2. The multimodal augmented feedback (visual-haptic) received during the training improves the participants’ learning curve in terms of performance measures compared to the unimodal (visual-only) augmented feedback.
H3. The augmented feedback (either uni or multimodal) received during the training decreases the participants’ perceived mental workload during the training phase compared to the control group.
H4. The multimodal augmented feedback (visual-haptic) received during the training decreases the participants’ perceived mental workload during the training phase compared to the uni-modal (visual-only) augmented feedback.
H5. The multimodal augmented feedback (visual-haptic) received during the training improves the perceived quality of collaboration between the participants and the instructor during the training phase compared to the uni-modal (visual-only) augmented feed- back.
H6. The improvement in performance between the pre- and post-tests is higher for the augmented feedback (either uni or multimodal) groups compared to the control group.
H7. The improvement in performance between the pre- and post-tests is higher for the multimodal augmented feedback (visual-haptic) group compared to the uni-modal (visual-only) augmented feedback group.
Concluding statement: "This study evaluated the impact of visual and visual-haptic feedback on the transfer of motor information during tool manipulation in a bimanual task within a collaborative virtual environment. The findings highlight the effectiveness of augmented feedback in enhancing motor performance and learning."
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: "While visual-haptic feedback did not improve trajectory quality or obstacle avoidance, it significantly reduced cognitive load and enhanced learners’ perceived collaboration with the expert."
Benefit_code: Haptic feedback improves collaborative performance and experiences
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: "the impact of such feedback and the different modalities used to communicate it remain underexplored in immersive virtual environments (IVE)."
Limitation_code: How participants reach the common ground?
C4.4_Future work: #VALUE!
Future_work_code: how to teach?, extend to AR

Title: Haptic Designation Tool to Improve Working Strategy in Collaborative Virtual Environment
Author: Girard, Adrien and Ammi, Mehdi
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: The paper evaluates a haptic tool for the designation of targets  in a context of collaborative molecular design where one user designates tasks and coordinates the actions of two operators.
C3.3.1_DVs: Code Performance etc:
Performance: Time, Score
Behavior: Social interaction
C3.4_Hypotheses_description:
H1. haptic feedback could play a role in conveying urgency of a user’s desire for control.
H2. haptic icons could be learned to a high degree of accuracy in under 3 min and remained identifiable even under significant cognitive workload
C3.4.1_Hypotheses_code: Haptic feedback in the interaction technique improves the communication of collaborative task.
Concluding statement:
The set of haptic icons developed was
usable, learned within 3 min and identified under workload
with 95% accuracy. In application simulation, users
utilized and responded to the icons appropriately, and task
performance was unaffected in this relatively brief task. Haptic
mediation appears to influence collaborative style, with
increased control turnover and more equitable time-in-
control. Users prefer multimodal feedback
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: Non intrusive communication/express different level of urgency/more fluid collaboration and less frustrating/
Benefit_code: Haptic feedback improves collaborative performance and experiences
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: limited capacity of off-the-shelf devices (frequency, maplitude, variations). subjective meaning of haptic interaction (is it urgent or not ?).
Limitation_code: not enough features for off-the-shelf haptic device. Haptic interaction is difficult to intepret.
C4.4_Future work:
Possible applications include meeting floor control, team document editing and design reviews, air controller. This deployment of haptic icons can also serve as an indicator of their potential use in non-collaborative situations such as computer-user
communication, where issues like graded levels of interruption and assignment of meaning to background signals also apply.
Future_work_code: Testing the system in different applications.

Title: Improvement of collaborative selection in 3D complex environments
Author: Girard, Adrien and Ammi, Mehdi and Simard, Jean and Auvray, Malika
C2.1_Haptic_device_(F): Phantom OR Geomagic (not mentionned, only photo)
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: Our work proposes to extend the use of haptic communication functions to 3D environments (i.e., molecular manipulation) presenting different communication constraints (e.g., distance, complexity of the environment).
C3.3.1_DVs: Code Performance etc:
Performance: Time, Score, Efficiency
Behavior: Movements, Position, Object manipulation
C3.4_Hypotheses_description:
H1. The collaborative metaphor will improve the collaborative selection of the same ROI for the redundant working strategy.
H2. The collaborative metaphor will improve the collaborative selection of different ROI for the complementary working strategy.
H3. The learning and understanding of the molecular environment is quicker with the collaborative metaphor.
Concluding statement: "The experimental results show that the combination of the haptic designation tool and the coordinator-operator working strategy improves the working efficiency and enables a better sharing of the activity between partners."
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: "we observe that the haptic guidance enables an accurate selection of targets which leads to an improvement of performance during the deformation process."
Benefit_code: Haptic feedback improves collaborative performance and experiences
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: N/A
C4.4_Future work: "Beyond the designation of targets, it would be interesting in future research to investigate collaborative tools for other kind of tasks."
Future_work_code: Explore in different types of task

Title: Collaborative metaphor for haptic designation in complex 3D environments
Author: Girard, Adrien and Auvray, Malika and Ammi, Mehdi
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: The paper presents a haptic guidance method to help a collaborator focus on a region of interest in CVE.
C3.4_Hypotheses_description:
H1. The haptic component of the metaphor reduces the global completion
time of the task.
H2. The haptic component of the metaphor reduces the reaction
time of the operator.
H3. The haptic component of the metaphor is more useful for tasks
involving numerous manipulations than tasks involving only
few manipulations.
C3.4.1_Hypotheses_code: Improve completion time/improve performance reduce mental load
Concluding statement: The results revealed the crucial role of the collaborative metaphor for an efficient and accurate designation of targets during the deformation procedure.
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: The collaborative haptic metaphor improve efficiency and accuracy without impacting completion time
Benefit_code: Haptic feedback improves collaborative performance and experiences
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: N/A
C4.4_Future work: The paper will study the role of the collaborative metaphor in the improvement of performance of tasks requiring the selection and the deformation of far regions.
Future_work_code: Test the technique in different tasks.

Title: Efficiency analysis in a collaborative task with reciprocal haptic feedback
Author: Groten, Raphaela and Feth, Daniela and Peer, Angelika and Buss, Martin and Klatzky, Roberta
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: The study compares efficiency in a collaborative mutual haptic feedback condition to three control conditions, where participants either acted alone or collaboratively without haptic feedback from the partner.
C3.2.1_Levels: Visual-haptic (with partner), visual (with partner), full-mass (alone), half-mass (alone)
Concluding statement:
The mutual haptic feedback condition required more effort
on a dyadic level than the vision feedback condition. herefore, hap-
tic feedback cannot be considered efficient in the present
interaction task. Compared to doing the task alone with
full mass, the vision-only interactive condition led to in-
creased efficiency. haptic feedback might not
always be of advantage. This is especially true when taking
into account the challenges from the engineering perspective.
However, haptic feedback leads to a fairer effort distribution
between the partners within a dyad compared to a vision-
only condition.
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: haptic feedback leads to a fairer effort distribution between the partners within a dyad compared to a vision-only condition.
Benefit_code: Haptic feedback improves collaborative performance and experiences
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: high variability in efficiency when haptic feedback is given, due to the different amount of interactive forces per dyad, leads to the conclusion that haptic feedback might not always be of advantage. The mutual haptic feedback condition required more effort on a dyadic level than the vision feedback condition. This is because the partners apply interactive forces in addition to the forces necessary to move the object.
Limitation_code: Participants need to learn the haptic collaboration.
C4.4_Future work: In future, we will analyze more complex collaborative tasks, based on the assumption that with a higher need for communication between partners haptic feedback will lead to increased efficiency due to this feedback channel. Furthermore, we will investigate the forces exchanged between partners (especially interactive forces) over time to get further insights into haptic collaboration
Future_work_code: Extend to complex tasks. Explore how collaborators reach a common ground.

Title: Exploring audio, visual, and tactile cues for synchronous remote assistance
Author: Günther, Sebastian and Kratz, Sven and Avrahami, Daniel and Mühlhäuser, Max
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: The study explored different communication modalities (audio,visual and haptic) for remote assistance (remote expert guiding a local guy).
C3.2.1_Levels: voice-communication only, audio signals, augmented labels, and tactile feedback
Concluding statement:
While visual cues in form of augmented labels improved their
communication by identifying objects faster while reducing the
number of spoken words, participants thought that tactile and audio
cues are interesting concepts improving communication but need to
be more precise
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: Tactile cue are intersting for their novelty and could be useful but in addition of other cues.
Benefit_code: Haptic feedback can be an alternative for communication
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: mixed reception of haptic TCT lower than other cues. lack precision and not really clear guidance
C4.4_Future work: we want to elaborate how isolated cues can be further improved, especially in more realistic scenarios, such as a car-repair with noisy environments. Therefore, we also want to find out how other constraints affect the team work through additional communication regulations and rules. Further, we want to explore how combinations of multiple cues assist during collaborative tasks and how a system can select cues depending on the context of a task.
Future_work_code: Test HC in more ecological setup

Title: User's behaviours in a collaborative task - real vs. virtual environments
Author: H. H. Le and M. J. Loomes and R. C. V. Loureiro
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: The paper investigates how different environments (real vs. virtual) and communication modalities (with vs. without talking to each other) affect the quality of interaction between humans
C3.3.1_DVs: Code Performance etc:
Performance: Time, Effort
Behavior: Physical response, Force applied, Position
C3.4_Hypotheses_description:
H1. The coordinated strategy improves the overall performance.
H2. The coordinated strategy improves the working efficiency.
H3. The coordinated strategy distributes the workload more evenly between the participants.
Concluding statement:
participants were more engaged
doing the collaborative task in the virtual environment using
haptic devices even though it was more difficult and
required them to put more effort than doing the same task in
the real world.
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: Haptic interface render the experience more fun
Benefit_code: Enhance experience
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: Task easier in real world than VR maybe because of haptic interaction
C4.4_Future work: investigate the difference in terms of performance and engagement between virtual and real environments for different types of group interaction in motor learning, such as a cooperation task (users have different roles) and a completion task (users have different goals).

Title: Haptic Cooperative Virtual Workspace: Architecture and evaluation
Author: Halabi, Osama and Horiguchi, S.
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: Validation of an architecture for an haptic cooperative workspace where the participants can kinestheticaily interact.
C3.3.1_DVs: Code Performance etc:
Performance: Time, Errors
Behavior: Force applied
Concluding statement: Using the proposed architecture, the system in cooperative mode provided up to 66% of the feeling that was produced by the single mode system
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: N/A
Benefit_code: N/A
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: N/A
C4.4_Future work:
Providing an evaluation approach for haptic systems will enable us to investigate the factors that increase the performance of the simulation independently and allow us to classify the haptic systems according to specific haptic devices and the nature
of the tasks undertaken.
Future_work_code: The need to evaluate haptic systems.

Title: Network latency issue in cooperative shared haptic virtual environment
Author: Halabi, Osama and Horiguchi, Susumu
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: The paper studies the influences of the network delay on the quality of haptic and the performance in a CVE.
Concluding statement: Cooperative shared haptic virtual environments enable the users to feel each other while interacting over the network.
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: N/A
Benefit_code: N/A
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: Delay does not impact participants to complete handshake, but increases time and reduces realism.
Limitation_code: Delay reduces collaborative performance and experience
C4.4_Future work: N/A

Title: Collaborative haptic environment assessment
Author: Hamza-Lup, Felix and Lambeth, Benjamin and LaPlant, James
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: The paper studies the effects of network latency on task performance
Concluding statement: Delay impairs collaboration, threshold found around 200ms
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: N/A
Benefit_code: N/A
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: N/A
C4.4_Future work: Adapt to more generic task
Future_work_code: Test HC in more ecological setup

Title: The evaluation of delay jitter for haptics collaboration over the Internet
Author: Hikichi, Kenji and Morino, Hironao and Arimoto, I. and Sezaki, Kaoru and Yasuda, Y.
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: The goal of this research is to clarify the quality of service requirement (network impairment) for a haptics collaboration system over the Internet.
Concluding statement: The experiment result indicates that the tradeoff between low network delay and little delayjitter is also true for haptics.
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: N/A
Benefit_code: N/A
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: N/A
C4.4_Future work: A perceptual study for compensation methods such as dead reckoning when facing network delay in CVE.

Title: Collaborative haptic play with building blocks
Author: Huang, Pingguo and Ishibashi, Yutaka and Fukushima, Norishige and Sugawara, Shinji
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: The paper examines the influences of  network latency and packet loss on collaborative work which deals with multiple objects in a 3-D virtual space.
Concluding statement: The paper found that the subjective rating of operatability decreased as the network delay increased. Co-manipulation received the best rating of operatability. However, these findings were descriptive without statistical models validating them.
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: N/A
Benefit_code: N/A
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: N/A
C4.4_Future work: The paper will investigate control methods to compensate/recover network delay

Title: Effects of Haptic Data compression on user performance in Collaborative Haptic Virtual Environment
Author: Huynh, Loi and Zadeh, Mehrdad
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: The goal of this study is to measure the effects of the haptic compression methods, statistical or perceptual techniques to render haptic signals when data communication creates heavy traffic, on user performance and perception.
C3.2.1_Levels: 1) position-based haptic data vs. position-force haptic data, 2) position-based haptic data with perceptual deadband, 3) position-based haptic data with perceptual deadband and perceptual extrapolation
Concluding statement: First, we examined the effects of compression techniques PBHD and PFHD on user performance. It concludes that PBHD and PFHD do not have a significant difference in terms of user performance. Afterwards, the effects of PBHD perceptual deadbands on user performance were studied. According to the results, higher deadband values create more task errors in the CHVE. Finally, the results show that the effect of PE on user perception is positive. However, the task errors were increased.
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: Finally, the results show that the effect of PE (position extrapolation) on user perception is positive.
Benefit_code: The proposed technique reduces the effect of delay.
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: However, the task errors of PE method were increased.
C4.4_Future work: Future work will focus on various estimation techniques that increase the power of the haptic compression method for different scenarios in a CHVE. In addition, rotation capturing to support action like drilling or sculpting will be implemented later on.
Future_work_code: Explore the proposed technique in different sceanrios.

Title: Simultaneous remote haptic collaboration for assembling tasks
Author: Iglesias, Rosa and Casado, Sara and Gutiérrez, Teresa and García‐Alonso, A. and Yu, Wai and Marshall, Alan
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: This paper proposes a collaborative virtual environment with haptic devices for assembly tasks.
Concluding statement: The haptic feedback obtained during remote assemblies with dependent collisions can continue to improve the sense of co-presence between users with regard to only visual feedback.
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: The system of this paper has an advantage in that it does not need to store and manage a history of movements.
Benefit_code: The proposed technique improves haptic collaboration.
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: PHANToM Omni can be limited to display weights and collisions.
C4.4_Future work: A future goal is to extend to more than 2 collaborators
Future_work_code: Scale up the number of collaborators

Title: HoloBots: Augmenting holographic telepresence with mobile robots for tangible remote collaboration in mixed reality
Author: Ihara, Keiichi and Faridan, Mehrad and Ichikawa, Ayumi and Kawaguchi, Ikkaku and Suzuki, Ryo
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: The paper introduces HoloBots, a mixed reality remote collaboration system that augments holographic telepresence with synchronized mobile robots
Concluding statement: mobile robots improve the UX globally
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: holobots significantly increases copresence and shared experience
Benefit_code: The proposed system improves haptic collaboration.
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: Scalability, user representation, robotic form factors
Limitation_code: The proposed system cannot represent many users.
C4.4_Future work: Force Aggregation: Combining multiple robots within a single shell could aggerate their force, enabling the remote user to move heavier objects for object actuation or provide stronger haptic feedback for haptic communication.

Title: Validation of a Human Cooperative Steering Behavior Model Based on Differential Games
Author: Inga, Jairo and Flad, Michael and Hohmann, Sören
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: The paper evaluates two control models for a human cooperative steering task.
C3.2.1_Levels: Differential game (DG, minimizing their individual cost function), disturbance attenuation (DA, the control action of the cooperating partnet is assumed to be a known disturbance of the system)
C3.4_Hypotheses_description: The differential game (DG) model can approximate the steering angle and steering velocity trajectories better than the disturbance attenuation (DA) model.
C3.4.1_Hypotheses_code: The proposed interaction technique is better than the other approach.
Concluding statement: The results highlight the fact that the differential game model is able to describe cooperative behavior of all partners without the knowledge of the future behavior of one of them.
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: Brings theoretical knowledge showing that a DG model suits better to joint haptic driving
Benefit_code: The proposed system improves haptic collaboration.
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: The measurement is computational demanding
C4.4_Future work: We will investigate the use of faster measures for differential games and the inclusion of submodels of the neuromuscular system of humans.

Title: Inter-stream synchronization between haptic media and voice in collaborative virtual environments
Author: Ishibashi, Yutaka and Kanbara, T. and Tasaka, Shuji
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: The paper presents a synchronization algorithm for haptic media and voice in CVEs.
Concluding statement: The algorithm can synchronize haptic and audio data.
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: N/A
Benefit_code: N/A
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: N/A
C4.4_Future work: Subjective measure, different task, voice and video.
Future_work_code: Explore different media, include subjective measure.

Title: Haptic Assistance Improves Tele-Manipulation With Two Asymmetric Slaves
Author: J. van Oosterhout and C. J. M. Heemskerk and H. Boessenkool and M. R. de Baar and F. C. T. v. d. Helm and D. A. Abbink
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: The paper aims to contribute to improving time-efficiency and safe handling for remote maintenance tasks that require two asymmetric robots controlled by either one user or two cooperators.
C3.4_Hypotheses_description:
H1. Assistance with a haptic link between subtasks improves task performance, requires less control activity and subjective workload, and increases acceptance and safety for both co-operated and uni-manual tasks. 
H2. For uni-manual operators, haptic assistance improves task performance up to, or even beyond, the performance of the co-operators.
Concluding statement: Haptic assistance improves task execution for co-operators and uni-manual operators.
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: By haptically linking control actions through a joint task environment, this assistance system significantly improves task efficiency, reduces physical and mental workload, and enhances coordination, ultimately allowing a single operator to match the performance levels of a collaborative pair.
Benefit_code: The proposed system improves haptic collaboration.
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: In haptic assitance, merging of forces could be confusing and difficult to interpret for the operator
Limitation_code: Participants need to learn haptic collaboration.
C4.4_Future work: Future research should explore how to adapt the support path towards two co-operating operators, identify which paradigms is more beneficial for asymmetric subtasks, identify the level of situation awareness and analyse the effect of interface design and haptic assistance on it, and include tasks with real slave hardware.
Future_work_code: Adapt the haptic support based on more measurements.

Title: Tele-Manipulation with Two Asymmetric Slaves: Two Operators Perform Better Than One
Author: J. van Oosterhout and C. J. M. Heemskerk and M. R. de Baar and F. C. T. van der Helm and D. A. Abbink
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: This experiment compares the standard approach of two co-operating operators that each control a single subtask, to a single operator performing bi-manual control over the two subtasks, and a uni-manual control approach.
C3.4_Hypotheses_description:
H1. Cooperators with reduced control > bi-manual
H2. Cooperators with reduced control < uni-manual
Concluding statement: When controlling two asymmetric slaves, a co-operating pair of operators performs better than a single operator.
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: not related to haptics : 2 user do better than 1
Benefit_code: Cooperation is better than single user
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: Expertise, learning or adapt during cooperation
Limitation_code: Participants need to learn haptic collaboration.
C4.4_Future work: Future research can study control behavior.

Title: Modeling the effects of delayed haptic and visual feedback in a collaborative virtual environment
Author: Jay, Caroline and Glencross, Mashhuda and Hubbold, Roger
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: This paper investigates the effects of network communication-induced latency in haptic and visual feedback from remote participants.
C3.2.1_Levels: 10 levels of latency were assessed in the experiment: 0, 25, 50, 75, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300, and 400 ms.
C3.4_Hypotheses_description: The hypothesis was thus that all measures of performance, whether haptic or visual, initiating or maintaining contact, actual task performance or users’ questionnaire ratings, would increase in direct proportion to the amount of latency.
C3.4.1_Hypotheses_code: Delay descreases the performance of haptic collaboration
Concluding statement: While questionnaire ratings and movement time increased from 50 ms latency in a linear manner, the rror percentage rose steeply from 25 ms but stagnated after 100 ms.
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: N/A
Benefit_code: N/A
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: Haptic suffers from network delay more than visual
C4.4_Future work: Further work can examine the effects of the cross-modal interaction and the relationship between latency and task difficulty or type.
Future_work_code: Explore other task under different delays

Title: Assessment of Environmental Effects on Collaborative Haptic Guidance
Author: Khademian, Behzad and Apkarian, Jacob and Hashtrudi-Zaad, Keyvan
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: This study investigates the effect of environmental factors (view points) on user performance
C3.2.1_Levels: View point; Path guidance; Dominance factor (of trainer); Path appearance
C3.3.1_DVs: Code Performance etc:
Performance: Time, Errors
Behavior: Force applied
C3.4_Hypotheses_description: The environment factor and geometry have significant effects on the performane of haptic collaboration, and whether there is an interaction effect.
Concluding statement: The environment geometry, tracing circle took longer and created more error compared to tracing square. Changing the environment viewpoint from top to fraont decreases task time in tracing square. Our experiments did not reveal any significant correlation between the dominance factor and the users’ tracking performance.
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: Path appearance, Viewpoint, Force-feedback guidance from environment have an impact on performance, differently each. They interact somewhat
Benefit_code: Haptic feedback improves collaborative performance and experiences
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: N/A
C4.4_Future work: Future work can consider gradual attenuation of guidance forces.

Title: Performance Issues in Collaborative Haptic Training
Author: Khademian, Behzad and Hashtrudi-Zaad, Keyvan
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: The paper presents a control model for direct interaction between a trainer and a trainee, as well as between the users and the virtual environment.
C3.2.1_Levels: The control of trainer over the trainee's movement
Concluding statement: The results show that when the alpha (dominance factor from the trainer) decreases, the performance error increases during the path tracing task.
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: The paper argues the proposed control method can be used for surgical training.
Benefit_code: Application
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: N/A
C4.4_Future work: Future work will focus on the use of filters rather than scalar for the dominance factor and the implementation of other haptic control architectures.
Future_work_code: Explore the dominance dynamic between collaborators

Title: Transatlantic Touch: A Study of Haptic Collaboration over Long Distance
Author: Kim, Jung and Kim, Hyun and Tay, Boon and Manivannan, M. and Srinivasan, Mandayam and Jordan, Joel and Mortensen, Jesper and Oliveira, Manuel and Slater, Mel
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: Experiment to evaluate the use of haptics in a collaborative situation over a network
Concluding statement: Haptics improve collaborative interaction.
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: Haptics is associated with an increase of the co-presence the use of direction probe reduces co-presence
Benefit_code: Haptic feedback improves collaborative performance and experiences
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: Delay
C4.4_Future work: Haptic interaction between humans and computers. A framework that guarantee stable connection in CVE with haptics.

Title: Collaborative Haptic Exploration of Dynamic Remote Environments
Author: Kim, Seokyeol and Park, Jinah
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: This paper implements a prototype that offers stable haptic interaction with real-world spatial data and impromptu guidance from a remote partner.
C3.4_Hypotheses_description:
H1. The proposed haptic guidance method increases the performance in remote exploration tasks than non-restrictive guidance.
H2. The proposed haptic guidance method increases the performance of remote exploration tasks when spatial information is visually limited or ambiguous than when it is visually clear.
H3. The proposed haptic guidance method increases the performance of remote exploration tasks when a target is moving than when it is static.
C3.4.1_Hypotheses_code: haptic guidance increase performance in target exploring tasks.
Concluding statement: We found significant improvement of task performance both in the path-following and the wiping task when haptic guidance was provided.
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: Haptics can support when visual is missing.
Benefit_code: Haptic feedback is an alternative communication when viusla is unavialable.
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: The prototype only provides single point contact.
Limitation_code: The proptotype has limited haptic feedback
C4.4_Future work: Combining multiple haptic feedback (force + thermal) or multi-touch.

Title: QoE Assessment of Single-User Operation and Dual-User Operation in Remote Robot Systems with Force Feedback
Author: L. N. Gassama and Y. Ishibashi and P. Huang
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: Is co controling a robot is more efficient subjectively and objectively for teleoperation task. What is the impact of teleoperation delay on this
Concluding statement: As the network delay becomes larger, the dual-user operation is more largely superior to the single-user operation.
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: If one person is delayed, the cooperatiive model still allows the control of the second collaborator.
Benefit_code: Cooperation is better than single user
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: Requires a lots of hardware
C4.4_Future work: Investigate objective measures

Title: Towards bare-hand interaction for whiteboard collaboration in virtual reality
Author: Liu, Guangtian and Su, Haonan and Wang, Jingyu and Qi, Qi and Sun, Haifeng and Zhuang, Zirui and Ren, Pengfei and Liao, Jianxin
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: The paper evaluates whether physical white board interaction can improve the efficiency of remote collaboration.
Concluding statement: Our user study indicates that Physical-writing achieves comparable performance to traditional whiteboard collaboration in terms of collaboration efficiency and outperforms Air-writing in terms of input accuracy and usability.
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: Lower workload
Benefit_code: Haptic feedback improves collaborative performance and experiences
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: Air writing may introduce fatigue. Size of the board can be limited
C4.4_Future work: N/A

Title: Networked multiplayer cooperative interaction using decoupled motion control method in a shared virtual environment with haptic, visual and movement feedback
Author: Liu, Guanyang and Lu, Keke
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: Can the motion of an avatar be decoupled with respect to a virtual environment coordinate system where one user independently controls one-dimensional movement of the avatar along one coordinate axis?
Concluding statement: Experimental results validate that movement (force) feedback is critical for users to successfully complete the task, without which it is nearly impossible for them to have good performances
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: V+H+M is better than other conditions in terms of performance and overall for co-presence
Benefit_code: Haptic feedback improves collaborative performance and experiences
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: Delay, requires compenstation methods
C4.4_Future work: Extending to more complex 3D tasks.

Title: Separate DOF control and mutual guidance in networked haptic collaboration maze game: Design and evaluation
Author: Liu, Lingzhi and Liu, Guanyang and Zhang, Yuru and Guo, Weidong and Lu, Keke and Zhou, Moyuan
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: The presented results motivated a new haptic collaboration mode (separate DOF control, SDC) in the fields of game design, education and cooperative assembly.
C3.4_Hypotheses_description:
H1. Performance increases with the haptic feedback because it makes the CVE more real compared to the visual feedback only.
H2. Co-presence is intense with the mutual guidance for the feeling of being guided intelligently.
H3. Attraction of the maze game increases for players because the new method which hasn’t experienced before.
Concluding statement: [Haptic mutual guidance] is an effective collaborative method and its tentative application in the internet game is attractive.
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: Improves co-presence and performance
Benefit_code: Haptic feedback improves collaborative performance and experiences
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: N/A
C4.4_Future work:
applying the collaboration to the
wide area network
Future_work_code: Test the system in more ecological setup

Title: Proxy Importance Based Haptic Retargeting With Multiple Props in VR
Author: Liu, Ziming and Wu, Jian and Wang, Lili and Li, Xiangyu and Im, Sio‐Kei
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: Haptic retargeting technique is proposed to establish the virtual-real correspondence by introducing an offset between the virtual hand and the real hand.
Concluding statement: In multi-user tasks, the use of the weighted proxy importance strategy proposed specifically for multi-user tasks significantly reduces the number of resets, the average hand movement distance, and the task completion time, compared to the direct use of the single-user method described earlier.
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: haptic feedback is crucial for enhancing user immersion as it complements the visual information received.
Benefit_code: Haptic feedback improves collaborative experience
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: the virtual world is dynamic and complex, and it is often challenging to replicate the same number and distribution of physical objects in the real world.
Limitation_code: Haptic props are not suitable for dynamice environment.
C4.4_Future work: In the future, we will consider adding predictions for user interaction sequences, for example, using models of the user’s gaze point and attention to estimate interaction sequences, which helps us to choose better props and placements.

Title: A study on haptic collaborative game in shared virtual environment
Author: Lu, Keke and Liu, Guanyang and L, Liu
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: The goal of the experiment is to assess the impact of force feedback on collaborative task performance.
Concluding statement: The results suggest that haptic feedback is beneficial for increasing the accurate rate and enhancing speed of collaborative task.
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: Shows benefits of haptic feedback for remote joint object manipulation.
Benefit_code: Haptic feedback improves collaborative performance
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: No statistical analysis in the paper.
C4.4_Future work: Future research direction is to address network delay and experiences in CVEs.

Title: The Effect of Haptic Delay on Robot-Mediated Dyadic Cooperation
Author: M. Du and S. Kager and B. Noronha and D. Campolo
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: Study to explore how haptic delay between humans influences their motor performance
Concluding statement: This study suggested that there is a reduced benefit (performance) from the connection for the Novice with a haptic delay.
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: This study could be beneficial for remote assistance between differently skilled participants, such as rehabilitation, where the impact of haptic delay remains uncertain.
Benefit_code: application
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: Time delays
C4.4_Future work: Extending to 2D robots (more complex haptic feedback)

Title: Effective Cooperative Haptic Interaction over the Internet
Author: M. Glencross and C. Jay and J. Feasel and L. Kohli and M. Whitton and R. Hubbold
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: We present a system that enables, for the first time, effective transatlantic cooperative haptic manipulation of objects whose motion is computed using a physically-based model.
C3.2.1_Levels:
- Local or wide area network
- Synchronization method
- Prediction of movement (On/Off)
C3.4_Hypotheses_description:
(Network Delay)
H1. there will be significantly less divergence of the mean position and orientation in the synchronized than in the unsynchronized case
H2. the synchronized-predicted case will reduce divergence further.
Concluding statement: We have described a method for maintaining synchronization between users in a peer-to-peer system. We demonstrate that minor differences in the subjective view between participants is not detrimental to performing a shared cooperative haptic task.
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: The inclusion of active or passive haptics in a virtual environment (VE) improves user experience by providing a reinforcing additional sensory cue, and in doing so overcomes the basic problem of there being ‘nothing to feel,’ as is the case in most VEs.
Benefit_code: Haptic feedback improves collaborative experiences
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: Cooperative haptic interaction over the Internet is especially challenging. Latencies over large distances cannot be avoided, and are typically much higher than levels at which human task performance is adversely affected.
C4.4_Future work: Further experiments are required to characterise performance on a wider variety of networks with higher latencies, or where jitter is more prevalent.
Future_work_code: Test the system in more ecological setup

Title: How to notify team members during asynchronous remote collaboration supported by mixed reality: Comparing visual, audio and tactile notifications
Author: Marques, Bernardo and Ferreira, Carlos and Silva, Samuel and Dias, Paulo and Santos, Beatriz Sousa
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc:
how distinct notifcation methods afect the consumption of MR instructions during asynchronous remote collaboration, 
i.e., how well they blend with the tasks being performed by on-site and remote team members.
Concluding statement: Tactile notifcations (C3) appear as the best possibility to call attention towards newer MR instructions during asynchronous remote maintenance, despite the participant’s role in the collaborative process.
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: Less effort to notifce
Benefit_code: Haptic feedback improves collaborative performance
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: Notification may not be perceived in the real-life scenario.
Limitation_code: In more complex and real-life scenario, participants may miss the notification.
C4.4_Future work: Spatial cues, more ecological scenario such as industry site
Future_work_code: Test the system in more ecological setup

Title: HapticPointer: a neck-worn device that presents direction by vibrotactile feedback for remote collaboration tasks
Author: Matsuda, Akira and Nozawa, Kazunori and Takata, Kazuki and Izumihara, Atsushi and Rekimoto, Jun
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: We designed a necklace-style device named HapticPointer for presenting a direction as pointing cues in remote collaboration tasks. The device can simulates the sensation of walking together. It is assumed that the sensation improves engagement between the local and remote users.
Concluding statement: Our user study indicated that vibrotactile feedback on the neck is a suitable way to provide direction. Moreover, our approach has the potential to convey deixis and to enhance the engagement between the local and remote users.
CS code: Haptic communication, Usability, Social interaction, Social presence
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: Non-verbal information, such as pointing cues and gazes, provide deixis, and it is an important part of communication. And haptic feedback can work without the need for visual feedback
Benefit_code: haptic as an alternative for communication
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: Limitation in DoF (since necklace) + necessitates 1 per user, not possible to share
Limitation_code: Require hardware for each user, difficult to deplay
C4.4_Future work: N/A

Title: Audio makes a difference in haptic collaborative virtual environments
Author: Moll, Jonas and Huang, Yingying and Sallnäs, Eva-Lotta
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: The paper studies if audio communication can compensate visual loss in haptic collaboration.
C3.4_Hypotheses_description: Audio cues can help a lack of visual feedback
C3.4.1_Hypotheses_code: Audio cues increase performance when missing visual.
Concluding statement: Results showed that sound cues together with haptic feedback made a difference in the interaction between the collaborators regarding their shared understanding of the workspace and the work process.
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: Sound cues together with haptic feedback made a difference in the interaction; maintaining awareness of ongoing work
Benefit_code: haptic as an alternative for communication
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: too simple, no spatial information
Limitation_code: The proposed haptic feedback is too limited.
C4.4_Future work: N/A

Title: A haptic tool for group work on geometrical concepts engaging blind and sighted pupils
Author: Moll, Jonas and Pysander, Eva-Lotta Sallnäs
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: The aim was to support collaborative learning among sighted and visually impaired pupils.
Concluding statement: Verbal communication was crucial for the work process but haptic guiding to some extent substituted communication about direction. The important lesson learned here is that haptic communicative functions can make a great contribution to both the overall collaboration and task solving process as well as the inclusion of the visually impaired pupil.
CS code: Haptic communication, Performance, Social presence, Affect
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: While guiding haptically, the group members do not need to talk as much about navigation in the environment and can focus more on solving the task.
Benefit_code: haptic as an alternative for communication
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: When the objects can be moved in a shared haptic environment visually impaired pupils do not get enough awareness of events in the shared workspace and the group resorts to verbalizing that information instead.
Limitation_code: How participants have different capability, they may reduce back to different communication channel.
C4.4_Future work: Researchers have only started to recognize that haptic feedback can be used to communicate information that is useful in a collaborative situation.
Future_work_code: Use haptic as an alternative for communication

Title: The Effects of Whole-Hand Interactions with One Fingertip Vibrotactile Feedback on Cooperative VR Game Experience and Performance
Author: Moon, Hye and Moon, Hayoun and Orr, Grady and Jeon, Myounghoon
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: We investigated the effects of interaction methods and vibrotactile feedback on users‚Äô social presence, presence, engagement, workload and performance in a cooperative VR game.

Title: Impacts of VR 3D sketching on novice designers’ spatial cognition in collaborative conceptual architectural design
Author: Rahimian, Farzad Pour and Ibrahim, Rahinah
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: This paper identifies the impact of using a haptic based VR 3D sketching interface for integrating novice designers, cognitions and actions to improve design creativity.
C3.4_Hypotheses_description:
H1. 3D sketching leads to an increase in cognitive activity
H2. decrement for percentage of the P-actions in 3D sketching sessions
H3. using 3D sketching interface will not increase the amount of the supporting P-actions, e.g. tracing over a drawing in manual works and changing 3D view in digital works.
H4. 3D sketching interface improves perceptual design activities.
H5. In 3D sketching sessions, the designers attend to their designed elements and spaces more than what they do in traditional sessions.
H6. In 3D sketching design sessions, the designers discover tacit design facts more than they do in traditional sessions.
H7. 3D sketching interface improves functionality and thoughtfulness of design activities.
H8. The reflections between design interfaces and the designers’ mind is rather high in 3D sketching design sessions compared with those in traditional design sessions.
H9. 3D sketching interface improves designers’ situative inventions during conceptual architectural design phases.
H10. In 3D sketching design sessions, design team members shared design ideas more frequently than what they did in traditional design sessions.
H11. In 3D sketching design sessions, design conversations are more argumentative and critical than those in traditional design sessions.
H12. 3D sketching interface stimulates designers’ gesture actions during conceptual architectural design process.
H13. 3D sketching design interface strengthens the links between designers’ gesture actions and their spatial reasoning.
C3.4.1_Hypotheses_code: 3D sketching improves cognitive abilities, cooperation, perception, creativity
Concluding statement: Results show that the simple and tangible haptic based design interface improved designers’ cognitive and collaborative activities.
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: Haptics improve collaboration and fosters creativity
Benefit_code: Haptic feedback improves collaborative performance and experiences
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: N/A
C4.4_Future work: Research findings from this study can help the development of cutting-edge haptic-based collaborative virtual environments in architectural education and associated professions.

Title: Mutual Skill Learning and Adaptability to Others via Haptic Interaction
Author: Saracbasi, Ozge and Harwin, William and Kondo, Toshiyuki and Hayashi, Yoshikatsu
Link: Saracbasi, Ozge and Harwin, William and Kondo, Toshiyuki and Hayashi, Yoshikatsu
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: The usage of robotic haptic interfaces generating force-field paved the way for understanding the human mechanism while learning skills through dyadic haptic interaction. Here haptic interaction is used as a way of studiyng transfer of technical skill while linked with a novice or an expert during learning (or also alone without a collaborator).
C3.4_Hypotheses_description:
H1. learning with another beginner would enhance motor learning as a result of peer-to-peer learning, adapting to the other's dynamics, and exploring the unknown environmental dynamics together.
H2. there should be a positive correlation between skill learning and adaptability, which means that the participants in Novice-to-Novice groups can adapt to each other by exploring the unknown environmental dynamics together, and motor skill learning can happen only when there is adaptation.
C3.4.1_Hypotheses_code: Peer-to-peer learning between two novice users enhances motor learning.
C3.6.1_Sample_size_description: 12 novices paired with the same novice, 8 novice paired with the same experimenter, 3 novice alone
Concluding statement: Our results showed that the experience with another novice partner during the Learning phase plays a significant role in adaptation to a new partner (adaptability) as well as a skill learning under an unknown field. That is to say, learning a motor task together with another novice through exploration of the unknown environmental dynamics led to higher adaptability for a person and to the best motor learning for a given task.
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: N/A
Benefit_code: N/A
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: N/A
C4.4_Future work: the principles of human-human interaction would facilitate the design of human-robot interfaces,

Title: Exploring Remote Collaborative Tasks: The Impact of Avatar Representation on Dyadic Haptic Interactions in Shared Virtual Environments
Author: Sasaki, Genki and Igarashi, Hiroshi
C2.1_Haptic_device_(F): robotic device simulating a physical plate. It is not the focus of the article (but a mean to investigate the research question).  So participants could manage two degrees of freedom. Mitsubishi Electric’s HG-MR43
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: Explore the interplay between haptic interaction and avatar representation in CVEs. Can haptic interaction alone generate sufficient social presence, or is visual avatar representation also required?
C3.2.1_Levels: Both the user’s and the partner’s avatars are displayed, only the user’s avatar is displayed, only the partner's avatar is displayed, no avatars are displayed
C3.3.1_DVs: Code Performance etc:
Performance: Score, Errors
Behavior: Force applied
C3.4_Hypotheses_description:
H1. Social presence is not affected by avatar rep (bc haptics)
H2. Performance is not affected by avatar rep (bc haptics)
H3. Effort/force applied is affected by avatar rep
C3.4.1_Hypotheses_code: Avatar rep influences effort applied, but not performance or social presence
Concluding statement: The results showed that participants felt a decrease in social presence when the partner’s avatar was not displayed. Meanwhile, task performance and effort remained constant under all conditions. These findings suggest that although haptic interaction alone does not fully compensate for the absence of visual cues in terms of social presence, it does not hinder task completion. Consequently, participants found the social presence provided by haptic interactions to be insufficient; however, the task execution was not affected by the representation of avatars.
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in:
- haptic interaction alone does not fully compensate for the absence of visual cues in terms of social presence,
- the task execution was not affected by the representation of avatars.
Benefit_code: Haptic vs avatar?emnodiment?
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: Not as strong as Visual FB (Avatar rep) for social presence
C4.4_Future work: Explore group size, richer communication channels, and different types of task
Future_work_code: Test with more users, more diverse task

Title: Investigating the Effects of Haptic Illusions in Collaborative Virtual Reality
Author: Weiß, Yannick and Rasch, Julian and Fischer, Jonas and Müller, Florian
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc: The paper investigates the impact of haptic illusions on multi-user collaboration in co-located XR environments.
C3.2.1_Levels: Shape and Size of virtual objects (changing the performance of haptic illusion)
Concluding statement: We found that visual-haptic mismatches disrupted collaboration, leading to reduced performance and user experience.
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in: Haptic Illusions (HIs) have been shown to enrich and extend the spectrum of haptic feedback which can be delivered by passive props
Benefit_code: Haptic feedback improves collaborative performance and experiences
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: When haptic illusion fails, the visual-haptic mismatches may decrease collaboration and user experience more.
C4.4_Future work: Future research needs to extend established haptic illusions to more ecologically valid contexts.

Title: Investigating effects of visual and tactile feedback on spatial coordination in collaborative handheld systems
Author: Yatani, Koji and Gergle, Darren and Truong, Khai
C3.1_Study_motivation_desc:
We investigated how visual and tactile feedback affects
collaboration on mobile devices, with emphasis on spatial
coordination in a shared workspace 2
C3.2.1_Levels:
Study1 : - tactile
- visual
- no feedback
Study 2 : 
- Visual
- tactile
- Visual+Tactile
Concluding statement:
- Visual feedback can provide precise spatial information
about collaborators, but can hamper collaboration when it
is occluded and sometimes distracts the user’s attention.
- Spatial tactile feedback can provide spatial information
about collaborators as well, but improvements are
necessary to convey precise spatial information. It can
reduce the overload of information in visual space and
can gently guide the user’s attention to an area of interest.
- Visual and spatial tactile feedback can complement each
other, and systems with both feedback channels can offer
better spatial coordination support than systems using
only one form of feedback
C4.2_Benefit of using haptic feedback in:
- Spatial tactile feedback can provide spatial information
about collaborators
- It can reduce the overload of information in visual space and
can gently guide the user’s attention to an area of interest.
Benefit_code: Haptic feedback improves collaborative performance and experiences
C4.3_Limitation of using haptic feedback: improvements are necessary to convey precise spatial information
C4.4_Future work: N/A
